sentences.text: My name is Fred Knoller.
Knoller, K-N-O-L-L-E-R.
The same.
Although my first name was Alfred.
No.
17th of April, 1921.
I'm 74 today.
Oh--
Not today, today.
I was born in Vienna in Austria.
Well, we came-- we were a middle-class Jewish family.
My father was an accountant, my mother a housewife.
Actually, my father worked for the same firm all his life.
He was actually born in former Austrian-Hungarian Empire, which was Lemberg, or Lwow, which it's called today.
And I think, I presume, that he went from one town in that empire to another town, which was Vienna.
And he was in the Austrian Army.
He showed us once a photo of him sitting on a horse in uniform, and somebody was holding the horse, which shouldn't move, and we were all laughing about it.
We are three brothers.
The oldest one is Otto, middle one is Eric, and me.
Life in Vienna was quite pleasant and-- and different from the life that we have today.
For instance, my parents were very musical, and they somehow made sure that the children played an instrument.
In fact, this was done in many, many families in Vienna.
My oldest brother plays the piano, middle brother learned the violin, and naturally I had to learn the cello.
So at the age of six, my parents got me into the-- with a teacher and-- to learn the cello.
And then I went, in the higher education, went to the music academy in Vienna.
And it was very pleasant, actually.
Because I remember every Friday night, this was music night.
We had Shabbos meal, Shabbat meal, and afterwards we sat down and we played.
We had chamber music and Viennese waltzes, and we played the latest-- the latest popular music, you know, just from-- by ear.
It was really pleasant.
I would say the entertainment that the children had at that time were so different from today, because I remember that we all went to other people's houses, and we played music.
Yes.
In fact, my parents insisted that every child had to practice half an hour, at least half an hour, a day.
So-- so when we came home from school, there was music in the house all the time for piano, violin, and then the cello.
Well, I took pleasure in playing but not in practicing.
This was somehow an-- an ordeal to go and play always the same thing and go over it and-- but naturally, today, I'm very happy, very glad, that I was, to a certain extent, forced to practice.
I went to school first to-- to a Volksschule, which was at the-- at the age up to 10 years of age, I think, 10 or 11, which was in [NON-ENGLISH] in Vienna, which was in the 2nd District.
The district where we lived was the 2nd District, but it was a Jewish district.
A lot of Jews lived there.
It wasn't a ghetto.
I mean, it was mixed people, but a lot of Jews lived there.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember my first experience of antisemitism was in the Volksschule, in that-- in that young school, when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when one of the other children from-- from a one class higher, maybe a year older than me, called me [NON-ENGLISH], you're a pig Jew.
And I just run away.
Later on, when I went to gymnasium at the age of 12-- I think it's 11, 12-- I experienced many, many more antisemitic attacks from children.
And we always-- [SIGHS] we Jews had to stick together, and we had to-- we fought them.
And one day, I really came home full of blood in my face because I got hit by-- by another schoolboy.
And my parents said to me, you mustn't fight.
If they say something, go away.
I said, no, this isn't right.
I mean, I want to-- I feel that I should really give back what they do to me.
One of the professors in the gymnasium, who was physical-- how do you call it?
He-- he was in charge of physical exercise-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Professor Meyer.
He was the greatest antisemite.
And he harassed the Jewish children if they couldn't climb up the ladder fast enough or on the rope or on-- and-- and-- and-- and jumping and-- and running and et cetera.
But he was the first real Nazi that I remember.
Because before Hitler came to invade it-- or no, he didn't invade-- annexed Austria, because he didn't invade it.
The Austrian people were quite happy to receive him.
Before a year-- before he already showed us a-- a-- a party member of the Nazi Party in Vienna before-- a year before he came.
I would say there were about 75% of Jews and 25% of Gentiles.
Oh, yes, yes.
Yes, yes.
Yeah.
Although we were in the majority, but this is why we had these fights with them.
And usually, the-- the-- the fights were closer to-- they were a little bit older than we were.
But well, apart from this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the life in Vienna was quite pleasant.
We went once a year always on holidays.
We went to Hungary on Lake Balaton, I remember.
And we went to Semmering, which is in the mountains, and-- and-- and-- but always only once a year for four weeks, never more than-- than that.
Yes, only.
I don't remember ever having had a Christian friend.
Or I don't remember that my parents had Christian friends.
Whenever we went to people's houses, they were always Jews.
I presume it was that hurt feeling, [? had, ?] you know, when-- to protect oneself.
One just didn't, because the country itself was terribly antisemitic, very antisemitic.
Oh, all the time.
All the time and very much.
And this was the reason, probably, the reason why we didn't have any Christian friends.
Not very.
I know my father had a seat in the synagogue.
He rarely went to synagogue during the years.
But on the high holidays, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, he went to the synagogue, and we went with him.
He-- we had to stand next to him in his-- in his seat and had to pray with him.
In fact, we-- we had a Jewish education through learning in-- in that school.
The school in gymnasium was split up.
The Christians had their own religious teaching, and the Jews had their own teaching.
And we learned Hebrew, to read Hebrew.
And-- and I don't think that we learned it to understand it or to-- to-- to-- but to read, yes.
We did read.
Yes, we had a bar mitzvah.
We had a bar mitzvah at home.
There were only our relatives there, the brother of my mother, and was the family Bodek.
We had a-- our very close friend of my parents, a neighbor of ours, Miss Schiff, Erna Schiff, and our family.
So it-- it was a family function at home, not a bar mitzvah like you had and-- where you invite thousands of people.
Well, at that time, I felt left out.
My oldest brother-- don't forget, my oldest brother, he was born 1913 on the 5th of April.
And he was-- 1913, he was eight-- eight years, is it?
I was born 1921.
Yes, so it's eight years older than me.
So when you are of the age of, let's say, 13 and he's 21, he's a man, nearly.
He went out with girls.
And naturally, I was too young.
[LAUGHS] My middle brother, Eric, was two years older than I am.
He was born in-- in 1920-- 1919.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] With the oldest brother I didn't have too much in common.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even with my two-year older brother, I didn't have really-- I was-- I felt left out, because what they were doing I couldn't do.
Because when you are that young, you're really the baby of the family.
In fact, I must say I looked extremely young, much younger than my years.
So when I was, let's say, 15, and-- and I saw some of my school friends going out with girls, I couldn't because I was-- I looked like 12.
I had a little bit of a complex maybe because of it.
I always looked young.
Today I'm very happy because I-- I-- I'm--
I still look young.
[LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The--
Well, we had-- we played football in-- in-- in parks and with-- with my school friends and-- I remember my parents, who were extremely gentle and very, very kind to us.
Although, I must say that if we did something wrong, we did get hit on our bottom, but when there was something really, really bad.
But mother always came and kissed us.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes, oh, yes.
There was-- once we played in-- in the-- in a park called the [NON-ENGLISH] Park on the Danube-- [? over. ?] That was our place where we went to play with.
And I-- I and some of my friends crawled up on a building where there was the swimming pool there, and I crawled up on the-- I went up on the roof.
And suddenly I heard the voice of my father, come down here.
And he said, how dare-- I went down-- and, how dare you?
Come home.
And he didn't do anything in front of my friends.
At home he said-- now, I knew what was coming, that I'm going to get smacks on my bottoms.
But he can do it quite well.
He said, don't you ever do that.
But before we went into the room where he was spanking me, I was putting a cushion in my bottom.
And he started hitting me, and then he felt the cushion.
So he laughed and he said, come on.
Don't you ever do that again, you know.
[LAUGHS] It's one of the instances that I remember.
[SIGHS] Yes.
We-- my-- the only other family-- grandparents I never knew.
The-- the family that we had was the Bodek family.
This is the father-- Herman Bodek was the brother of my mother.
This family-- later on when we come to it, I will-- you will see we were together in Belgium and in France.
And I'm still very, very friendly with them today.
They live in France, actually.
There were, again, three children.
One was Rosie.
She was age of my oldest brother.
And then there was Leo.
He died last year, actually.
Leo, he was my middle brother's age.
And then the young is Maxwell, Max.
He was my age.
So I was quite friendly with my cousin Max.
And he-- he-- he had a bicycle-- I didn't have a bicycle-- because they lived a little bit outside Vienna.
And he let me-- he lent me the bicycle quite often.
And we-- we speak to each other today still over the telephone.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] Well, what actually happened-- it was the 11th of March, 1919-- 1938.
It was a Friday night, Friday-- I think it was Friday-- when we heard that the German entered Austria.
I remember the time-- Schuschnigg was in power.
And over the radio he announced that he felt that he's giving up the power and-- and please do not fight the Germans.
They want to come in.
He was against the occupation, but he didn't want to have bloodshed.
And he said, I don't want to create any bloodshed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
One hour-- you know, we lived in an apartment house.
We lived on the third floor.
And every apartment house had his-- his housekeeper.
[NON-ENGLISH] they called him.
And our [NON-ENGLISH] was-- I was quite-- he was not Jewish-- I was quite friendly with him.
Why?
Because he-- like me he was a stamp collector.
We-- we exchanged stamps, and we got together quite often.
I went to his home, his house downstairs in the cellar, and we exchanged stamps.
And he was always-- whenever we talked somehow or other about politics, he was very left wing-- seemed to be left wing.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he-- I believe he supported the Socialist Party, because I myself, was somehow left wing-inclined when I was a student.
In fact, I was a member of the [NON-ENGLISH].
That is the association of the socialist students.
And there was never any antisemitic remarks from him or-- he was always left wing, as far as I was concerned.
On the day after the invasion, the 11th of March-- actually, this was my brother's birthday, Eric-- he came up with an insignia of the Nazi Party, and he had a swastika band.
And when we passed each other in the building, he said, well, what do you-- what-- what can I do?
That's all he said.
What-- what can I do?
So he more or less indicated he was forced to do to show his pro-German attitude.
Otherwise-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] well, this is an instance.
How did that-- did that then affect his relationship with you?
I mean, what happened to your relationship with him?
Well, we did not anymore-- we did not anymore exchange stamps.
There's one instance-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Germans entered Austria in March.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And things were extremely, extremely difficult for Jews because the-- the law, Nuremberg laws, et cetera, were already in-- in-- in Austria-- applied for Austria also.
The Nazis, the Austrian Nazis, were tremendously-- it was tremendously anti-Jewish.
I think three months after-- March, April, May, maybe-- maybe June-- the law came out that Jewish students couldn't be educated anymore, but they had to learn a trade.
So when-- my parents entered me into a tailoring school.
My oldest brother, who was studying in university-- he was studying medicine at that time-- he could not continue anymore.
My middle brother could not continue his education.
But it was extremely difficult for us Jews to go out in the street and-- and maybe do whatever we wanted to do.
Because at that time, right after the invasion, at the time when Schuschnigg was actually in power, a lot of left-wing slogans were painted all over the pavement, all over the walls.
Placards were posted up, anti-Nazi, especially in our neighborhood.
And I remember that the SR picked up people from the street in order to clean the slogans, communist or socialist slogans, from the street.
And we were always afraid to-- to be part of it, to be asked to-- to clean.
One day I did go.
I had to go out to get something, to a shop, and I saw a group of people.
Curious as I am, I went there to have a look what's-- what's there.
There was an-- I'll never forget it-- there was an very old Jew with a beard.
I think he must have been somewhere around 60 or 70 years of age.
And they gave him-- they gave him water and a toothbrush, and he had to clean the slogan with a toothbrush.
And it wasn't good enough, and they kicked him while he did it.
And you can see-- I remember the faces of the people who surrounded him laughing, ha-ha, you know, dirty Jew and-- and-- and he-- he wore a hat.
And underneath the hat he wore a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how you call it, a--
A kippah-- skull--
Kippah, yes.
Skullcap, yeah.
And the hat fell down, and they wanted to pick it up.
They pick-- they-- they played football-- football with it.
I mean, one S-- SR-- it was the SR-- man in brown uniform with the swastika [INAUDIBLE], gave him a kick in-- in his face.
And blood was streaming down, and he had to go and continue cleaning.
[SIGHS] I will never, ever forget this.
This was so inhuman.
And it was really the first experience that I had of unjustified violence against a human being, from one human being to another one.

And since the time, my parents really said, well, only when you go to school-- not the-- the tailoring [INAUDIBLE], and I don't want you to go and-- and-- and go or out walking in the street, et cetera.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I may come back a little bit on the-- when the German went-- entered, the troops entered.
It was the 11th of March.
And before that, the night before, there were always anti-- anti-- anti-Nazi demonstration by the communists and by the socialists, because there was a very large contingent of-- of left-wing people.
And they went-- in Vienna, the biggest street is the Ring.
And there were always parades and red flags and-- and-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thousands and thousands of people went there to-- and my brothers and I also joined.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the 11th of March, as the Germans entered, we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was once in the street, and I saw the German Army or troops or [?
SR ?] parading, marching, in our street, which was the Untere Augartenstrasse.
We lived number 32.
And you saw hundreds of people, just right after the invasion, the-- the people who were left wing with their arms stretch out-- Heil Hitler, et cetera, becoming Nazi.
And the people were in the streets, you know, all swastika.
All-- every house had a swastika.
You know, it-- it seemed to me so unrealistic that one day they were socialists, the next day they were Nazis.
And they were-- enjoyed the troops and the [?
SR ?] from Germany.
A very frightening time.
My parents wanted to make sure, at that time, because we knew exactly what has happened in Germany with the Jews, you know, how they were persecuted.
And they couldn't have businesses, et cetera, and-- and they were arrested and taken to camps.
And-- and somehow or other, they told us, when we had me at home, that, look, you children, you got to go.
You have to leave the country.
There's no doubt you have to leave.

Our closest friend, my parents' closest friend, Erna Schiff, who lived absolutely right next door to our apartment on the same floor, she says, look, I have a brother-- no, I have a cousin, not a brother, but I have a cousin in America.
I'm going to ask him to send for one of the children a-- an affidavit for him to come.
And she put things in-- in motion, and it was decided that my middle brother, Eric, was the first one to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he actually went before the Kristallnacht.
So therefore, it must have been somewhere around in August or September, he got the affidavit.
He got a visa, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he went off to America.
Not really.
It was the decision of my parents that-- he was the middle brother-- that he must go.
And he was at the age, at that time, of 19-- 1938, yes, and he was 19.
My older brother, who was a medical student, thought that he will be able-- was working on it-- that he will be able with the children transport to England.
And that was because they were looking for people to accompany these children, and as he was a medical student it was possible that he will go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] me, I queued up at many embassies, at many embassies, to try to find a-- to try to find a-- a country that would accept me as an immigrant.
And I-- I went actually out to-- to, I remember, a Cuban embassy, Cuban embassy, Shanghai, countries I never heard of in South America and queued up sometimes all night long.
My parents said, look, the children, you must go.
But nothing really-- we are too old-- nothing will happen to us.
But you must create a new life outside the country.
And I was not able to get a-- anywhere a visa, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't-- I wasn't able to go.
